evals.name: express-docgen
skill_advantages: Created separate helper module (src/lib/generateDocx.ts) with TypeScript interface
Created separate route file (src/routes/docx.ts) following Express Router pattern
Properly wired route into main app via import + app.use()

evals.name: nextjs-docgen
skill_advantages: Created separate helper module (lib/generate-docx.ts) with comprehensive options interface
Used correct default import (HTMLtoDOCX) matching package's export
Without skill used wrong named import { htmlToDocx } — would fail at runtime

evals.name: standalone-script
skill_advantages: Richer HTML demo content (tables, lists, features showcase)
evals.notes: Both passed all assertions. Without-skill used wrong option name 'margin' instead of 'margins' which may cause issues at runtime.

key_findings: Skill ensures correct default import syntax (HTMLtoDOCX) vs without-skill guessing named imports
Skill consistently creates modular architecture (helper + route/handler) vs inline code
Skill uses correct option names from the API reference (margins, fontSize in half-points)
Without-skill standalone was surprisingly good — simple script tasks don't benefit as much from skill guidance
Biggest skill uplift is on framework integration (Express, Next.js) where modularity and correct API usage matter most
